data.summaryContent:
<overview>
The task was to implement GitHub issue #143 ("Bring every detected agent to full support parity") in the wasa-cli repository, which required filling capability gaps for `codex`, `gemini`, `cursor-agent`, and `aider` across three seams: config-dir isolation, autonomy flags, and session recording — all built on the unified `agent.Agents` registry introduced by the prerequisite issue #142 (already merged). The approach was to research each agent's real CLI flags/env vars (verifying against official docs/source rather than assumptions), fill in `internal/agent/agent.go` fields, implement a new `aider` recorder that works despite aider having no hook mechanism, and add/strengthen tests and documentation — while being careful not to introduce regressions in unrelated launch behavior.
</overview>

<history>
1. User provided a GitHub issue URL (https://github.com/JoakimCarlsson/wasa-cli/issues/143) with no further instructions.
   - Fetched issue #143 via `gh api` (title/body) after `gh issue view` returned empty output unexpectedly.
   - Fetched blocking issue #142 and confirmed it was CLOSED/merged (PR #154 "agent: unify per-agent registries into one source of truth").
   - Explored the current `internal/agent` package (`agent.go`, `agent_test.go`, `doc.go`) to understand the unified registry data model introduced by #142.
   - Explored `internal/record` (recorder.go, claude.go, codex.go, gemini.go, cursor.go, hooks.go, handle.go, message.go) and `internal/profile/profile.go`, `internal/launch/autonomous.go` to understand how `agent.Agents` fields drive each seam.

2. Researched real CLI details for codex, gemini, cursor-agent, and aider (avoiding hallucinated web-search answers by cross-checking against actual source/docs).
   - Confirmed `CODEX_HOME` and `GEMINI_CONFIG_DIR` are already used in-repo (`record/codex.go`, `record/gemini.go` via `agentHome()`), validating them as real env vars.
   - Fetched official cursor.com CLI docs (`/docs/cli/reference/parameters`) — confirmed only `CURSOR_API_KEY` is documented, no config-dir override exists.
   - Fetched aider's official docs (aider.chat/docs/config/options.html) and searched aider's GitHub source (via `github-mcp-server-search_code`/`get_file_contents`) — confirmed: `--yes-always` flag (env var `AIDER_YES_ALWAYS`, no alias), no `AIDER_HOME`-style config-dir env var (config resolved via `.aider.conf.yml` search path), and the exact chat-history format from `aider/io.py` (`append_chat_history`, `user_input`, `ai_output` methods) — lines prefixed `"#### "` are user turns, unprefixed lines are assistant output, `"> "` lines are blockquoted tool output, banner line `"# aider chat started at ..."` marks session start.

3. Implemented the registry and recorder changes.
   - Edited `internal/agent/agent.go`: added `ConfigDirVar: "CODEX_HOME"` to codex, `ConfigDirVar: "GEMINI_CONFIG_DIR"` to gemini, left cursor-agent's `ConfigDirVar` empty with an explanatory comment, and gave aider `Autonomy: &Autonomy{Flag: "--yes-always"}` and `RecorderTool: "aider"` (ConfigDirVar left empty with rationale comment).
   - Created `internal/record/aider.go` implementing the `Recorder` interface: `InstallHooks`/`RemoveHooks`/`HooksInstalled` are no-ops (aider has no hook mechanism), `LocateTranscript` resolves `.aider.chat.history.md` at repo root (ignoring session id), `TranscriptTarget` returns `""` (not session-keyed), `ResumeArgs` returns `nil` (no native resume), and `Normalize`/`Intent` parse the markdown chat log via a new `normalizeAiderHistory` helper.
   - Registered `aiderRecorder{}` in `recorders` slice in `internal/record/recorder.go`.
   - Modified `internal/record/handle.go`'s `Finish()` function to add a fallback: when no transcript was found via hook-reported state, it directly calls `recorderFor(m.Agent).LocateTranscript("", repoDir)` (bypassing the session-id-required `fallbackTranscript` helper) so hookless agents like aider can still have their transcript picked up at `wasa finish` time.

4. Ran build/tests and fixed a broken pre-existing test.
   - `go build ./...` succeeded.
   - `go test ./...` revealed `TestByRecorderTool` failure in `internal/agent/agent_test.go` (it asserted aider had no recorder — now false) and one pre-existing environment-flaky failure `TestPushToOrigin` (confirmed via `git stash` that this fails identically on the unmodified baseline branch due to no network/origin remote in the sandbox — unrelated to changes).
   - Fixed `agent_test.go`: updated `TestByRecorderTool` to expect aider now resolves to a recorder, and extended `TestConfigDirVarAliasing` with cases for codex, gemini, cursor-agent, and aider.
   - Added a new stricter parity test `TestCapabilityGapsAreDeclaredExplicit` in `agent_test.go` with a `declaredConfigDirGapReasons` allowlist map (currently containing `cursor-agent` and `aider`), asserting every declared agent has `ConfigDirVar` implemented-or-allowlisted, and `Autonomy`/`RecorderTool` always populated (satisfying the issue's acceptance criterion about tightening the parity test).
   - Wrote `internal/record/aider_test.go` with tests: `TestAiderRecorderNoHookMechanism`, `TestAiderRecorderLocateTranscript`, `TestAiderNormalizeAndIntent`, and `TestFinishAiderPicksUpChatLogWithoutHooks` (verifying the new `Finish()` fallback works end-to-end: intent extracted, transcript non-empty, "no hook data received" gap present but "transcript unavailable" gap absent).
   - Updated `README.md`'s "Session recording" section: added a paragraph about aider's hookless fallback-via-finish behavior, and a new "Per-agent capabilities" subsection with a markdown table summarizing config-dir var / autonomy flag / recorder status per agent.

5. Ran `make lint` (clean, 0 issues) and `go test ./...` again (only the known pre-existing `TestPushToOrigin` flake failed).
   - Ran `make fmt` (goimports + golines) which reformatted some *unrelated* files (`internal/tui/app_test.go`, `internal/tui/checkpoint_search_test.go`, `internal/tui/theme/theme_test.go`, `internal/record/agent_registry_test.go`) due to pre-existing long lines unrelated to this change.
   - Reverted those unrelated files with `git checkout --` to keep the diff scoped to this task, then re-ran `make lint`, `make build`, and `go test ./...` to confirm everything still passed cleanly.

6. Discovered and fixed a behavioral regression risk: since aider now has a `RecorderTool`, `record.AgentForProgram("aider ...")` now returns `true`, which would cause `internal/launch/launch.go`'s `seedPrompt()` to seed aider with a positional-argument prompt (previously blocked because aider had no RecorderTool). Verified via aider's docs that positional args mean "add this file to context," not "send this message," and that `--message`/`-m` is a one-shot flag that exits chat mode entirely — neither fits wasa's "seed the first message of an interactive session" contract.
   - Fixed by adding an explicit `if baseExe(program) == "aider" { return "" }` guard in `seedPrompt()` in `internal/launch/launch.go`, with a doc comment explaining aider has a recorder but no CLI seeding mechanism.
   - Extended `TestCreateSessionSeedsAgentPrompt` in `internal/launch/launch_test.go` with a new case verifying aider is spawned bare (`"aider"`) even when `InitialPrompt` is set, mirroring the existing shell-bypass case.
   - Ran `go build ./...` and the targeted test — both passed.
</history>

<work_done>
Files created:
- `internal/record/aider.go`: New `aiderRecorder` implementing the `Recorder` interface for aider — no-op hooks (aider has none), `LocateTranscript`/`TranscriptTarget`/`ResumeArgs` tuned to aider's lack of per-session addressing, and `normalizeAiderHistory` parsing aider's markdown chat log format (`.aider.chat.history.md`) into `Message` structs.
- `internal/record/aider_test.go`: Tests for the aider recorder's no-op hook behavior, transcript location, markdown normalization/intent extraction, and the `Finish()` fallback pickup end-to-end.

Files modified:
- `internal/agent/agent.go`: Added `ConfigDirVar` for codex (`CODEX_HOME`) and gemini (`GEMINI_CONFIG_DIR`); added explicit "no config-dir" comment for cursor-agent; added `Autonomy` (`--yes-always`) and `RecorderTool` (`"aider"`) for aider, with rationale comments for all declared absences.
- `internal/agent/agent_test.go`: Fixed `TestByRecorderTool` (aider now has a recorder), extended `TestConfigDirVarAliasing` with new cases, added new `TestCapabilityGapsAreDeclaredExplicit` test with `declaredConfigDirGapReasons` allowlist.
- `internal/record/recorder.go`: Added `aiderRecorder{}` to the `recorders` slice.
- `internal/record/handle.go`: Added a fallback transcript lookup in `Finish()` for hookless agents (calls `recorderFor(m.Agent).LocateTranscript("", repoDir)` when no transcript was found via hook state).
- `internal/launch/launch.go`: Added an explicit aider exclusion in `seedPrompt()` to prevent broken positional-prompt seeding.
- `internal/launch/launch_test.go`: Extended `TestCreateSessionSeedsAgentPrompt` with an aider case.
- `README.md`: Updated "Session recording" section with aider's fallback-only recording explanation, and added new "Per-agent capabilities" table/subsection.

Verification performed:
- `go build ./...` — passes.
- `make lint` (go vet + golangci-lint) — 0 issues.
- `go test ./...` — all pass except `TestPushToOrigin` (confirmed pre-existing, environment-only flake unrelated to these changes — fails identically on unmodified baseline due to no network access to git origin in this sandbox).
- Ran `./bin/wasa --help` as a basic sanity check after `make build`.

Current state: The core registry/recorder/docs work is complete and verified. The aider launch-seeding regression was caught and fixed. The last action taken was running `go build ./...` and the targeted `TestCreateSessionSeedsAgentPrompt` test after the `seedPrompt` fix — both passed. A full `go test ./...` and `make lint` re-run after this last fix has NOT yet been done and should be done next to confirm nothing else broke.
</work_done>

<technical_details>
- **Agent registry model** (`internal/agent/agent.go`): `Agent` struct fields (`ConfigDirVar`, `ConfigDirAliases`, `Autonomy *Autonomy`, `RecorderTool`) use zero-value (empty string/nil) as an intentional "explicitly declared absence" — this was decided in the prerequisite issue #142, not something to change. Comments in `agent.go` document the rationale for each absence; my new `TestCapabilityGapsAreDeclaredExplicit` test additionally encodes this in a Go-checkable allowlist (`declaredConfigDirGapReasons`) so future gaps can't slip through unnoticed.
- **Verified env vars via source-of-truth, not web search**: Generic web searches hallucinated plausible-but-false env vars (e.g., `AIDER_HOME`, `CURSOR_CONFIG_DIR`) — always cross-checked against either the wasa-cli codebase's own existing usage (`agentHome("CODEX_HOME", ...)`, `agentHome("GEMINI_CONFIG_DIR", ...)`) or the agent's actual GitHub source/official docs before trusting a claim.
- **Aider has no hook mechanism**: confirmed via aider's actual source and docs — its only lifecycle callback is `--notifications-command` (fires once per response, no event/session payload). This is fundamentally different from claude/codex/gemini/copilot/cursor, all of which have structured hook configs.
- **Aider's chat log format** (`.aider.chat.history.md`, from `aider/io.py`): user input lines are written prefixed with `"#### "` (one prefix per input line, joined with markdown soft-break `"  \n"`); AI output is written raw/unprefixed; a session-start banner is `"# aider chat started at <timestamp>"`; tool/command output during the session is blockquoted with `"> "`. This is a single shared file per directory, NOT keyed by session id — a genuine limitation that shaped `TranscriptTarget`/`ResumeArgs` returning empty/nil.
- **`Finish()` fallback subtlety**: The existing `fallbackTranscript()` helper requires a non-empty `sessionID` to look anything up, which doesn't fit aider (no session-id-addressable transcript). Rather than modifying that shared helper (used by the live hook-event path for other agents), a separate, more permissive fallback was added directly in `Finish()` that calls `recorderFor(agent).LocateTranscript("", repoDir)` — this only actually finds something for agents whose `LocateTranscript` implementation ignores the session-id argument (currently gemini and aider); for session-keyed recorders (claude, codex, cursor, copilot) it's a safe no-op (builds a nonexistent path, `existing()` returns `""`).
- **Critical regression caught late**: Because `agent.AgentForProgram()` (used by both `record.InstallHooks` wiring AND `launch.seedPrompt`'s "can this agent take a positional prompt" check) is keyed off `RecorderTool != ""`, giving aider a `RecorderTool` silently changed `seedPrompt`'s behavior too — a coupling that wasn't obvious from the recorder work alone. Aider's CLI has no way to seed an ongoing interactive session's first message (positional args add files; `--message`/`-m` is one-shot and exits chat mode), so a positional prompt seed would be actively wrong for aider (interpreted as a file path argument). Fixed via an explicit `baseExe(program) == "aider"` early-return in `seedPrompt`. This also means `wasa session resume` on an aider session will now silently drop the checkpoint preamble (since `seedPrompt` zeroes `InitialPrompt` for aider even when resume sets it) — this is correct/honest behavior given aider's constraints, but the resume CLI's success message ("resuming from checkpoint record...") doesn't currently distinguish this case; left as-is since it's a minor edge case not covered by explicit acceptance criteria, but worth knowing about if revisited.
- **`make fmt` side effects**: running `make fmt` (goimports + golines) reformatted unrelated pre-existing long lines in files never touched by this task; these were reverted via `git checkout --` to keep the diff scoped. Be cautious running `make fmt` again — it may re-touch those files; only commit intentional changes.
- **`TestPushToOrigin` is environment-flaky, not a regression**: confirmed via `git stash` that it fails identically on the unmodified base branch — the sandbox has no reachable git `origin` remote.
</technical_details>

<important_files>
- `internal/agent/agent.go`: Single source of truth for agent capabilities. Modified to add `ConfigDirVar` for codex/gemini and `Autonomy`/`RecorderTool` for aider, with explanatory comments for every declared absence (cursor-agent and aider's `ConfigDirVar`).
- `internal/agent/agent_test.go`: Contains `TestCapabilityGapsAreDeclaredExplicit` (new) — the strengthened parity test per issue acceptance criteria; also `TestByRecorderTool` and `TestConfigDirVarAliasing` (updated for new capabilities).
- `internal/record/aider.go`: New recorder implementation — the core deliverable for acceptance criterion #1 (aider recording). Read this fully before making further changes to it.
- `internal/record/aider_test.go`: New tests validating the recorder and the `Finish()` fallback integration.
- `internal/record/recorder.go`: `recorders` slice (line ~67-74) — aider added; `AgentForProgram` (line ~90) is the function whose behavior change (aider now returns `ok=true`) caused the launch-seeding regression that had to be fixed separately.
- `internal/record/handle.go`: `Finish()` function (~line 265-337) — added the hookless-agent transcript fallback right after the existing `st.TranscriptPath` read (~line 314-330).
- `internal/launch/launch.go`: `seedPrompt()` function (~line 180-199) — added the aider exclusion; this is the fix for the regression discovered late in the session. Should be double-checked for any other similarly-coupled assumptions on `AgentForProgram`/`RecorderTool != ""` if more work continues here (e.g., `installRecordHooks` at line ~126 also calls `AgentForProgram` but is safe since `InstallHooks` on aider is a no-op).
- `internal/launch/launch_test.go`: `TestCreateSessionSeedsAgentPrompt` (~line 266-297) — extended with the aider bare-spawn case.
- `README.md`: "Session recording" section (~line 153-230) — added aider fallback explanation and new "Per-agent capabilities" table.
- `internal/cli/resume.go`: NOT modified, but relevant — `planResume`/resume flow (~line 100-163) is where the aider seeding-drop-on-resume side effect surfaces; flagged as a known minor gap, not fixed.
</important_files>

<next_steps>
Remaining work:
1. Re-run the full verification suite after the `seedPrompt` aider fix, since only a targeted test was run after that last change: `go build ./...`, `make lint`, and `go test ./...` (expect only the pre-existing `TestPushToOrigin` flake to fail).
2. Double check `git status`/`git diff --stat` to confirm the diff is still scoped correctly (no unrelated files re-touched, e.g. if `make fmt` is run again).
3. Consider whether to check other callers of `record.AgentForProgram`/`agent.ByExe(...).RecorderTool != ""` in the codebase (e.g., `internal/launch/launch.go:126` `installRecordHooks`, `internal/launch/autonomous.go`, `internal/tui/modal/create.go`) for any other implicit assumptions that aider's newly-nonzero `RecorderTool` might silently break — a quick `grep -rn "AgentForProgram\|RecorderTool" internal --include=*.go` sweep would be prudent before considering this done.
4. Decide whether the resume-flow honesty gap (checkpoint preamble silently dropped for aider on `wasa session resume`) warrants a small message tweak in `internal/cli/resume.go`, or whether it's acceptable to leave as-is given the issue's acceptance criteria don't explicitly test resume behavior for aider.
5. Once all verification passes cleanly, the work satisfies the issue's stated acceptance criteria (aider recorder + finish-time transcript capture, codex/gemini config-dir vars, aider autonomy flag, strengthened parity test, README docs, `make lint`/`make test` passing) — the user has not yet been given a final summary/completion message, and no PR has been opened (the issue's "How to verify" section mentions opening a PR only after real-world validation with actual agent binaries, which is out of scope for this sandboxed session — this should be flagged to the user rather than assumed).
</next_steps>

<ask_user>
Use the ask_user tool to ask the user clarifying questions when needed.

**IMPORTANT: Never ask questions via plain text output.** When you need input from the user, use this tool instead of asking in your response text. The tool provides a better UX and ensures the user's answer is captured properly.

Guidelines:
- Prefer multiple choice (provide choices array) over freeform for faster UX
- Do NOT include "Other", "Something else", or similar catch-all choices - the UI automatically adds a freeform input option
- Only use pure freeform (no choices) when the answer truly cannot be predicted
- Ask one question at a time - do not batch multiple questions
- Don't ask the questions in bullet points or numbered lists. Ask each question in a clear sentence or paragraph form.
- If you recommend a specific option, make that the first choice and add "(Recommended)" to the label
  Example: choices: ["PostgreSQL (Recommended)", "MySQL", "SQLite"]

Examples:
1. BAD - bundling multiple questions into one and asking the user to confirm or break them apart:
  { "question": "Here's what I'm thinking:\n1. Use PostgreSQL for the database\n2. Add Redis for caching\n3. Use JWT for auth\nDoes this sound good, or would you like to discuss each choice individually?", "choices": ["Sounds good", "Let's discuss individually"] }
  WORKAROUND - ask one focused question per tool call:
  First call:  { "question": "What database should I use?", "choices": ["PostgreSQL", "MySQL", "SQLite"] }
  Second call: { "question": "Should I add Redis for caching?", "choices": ["Yes", "No"] }
  Third call:  { "question": "What auth strategy should I use?", "choices": ["JWT", "Session-based", "OAuth"] }
2. BAD - embedding choices in the question text instead of using the choices field:
  { "question": "What database should I use? (PostgreSQL, MySQL, or SQLite)" }
  WORKAROUND - put the options in the choices array:
  { "question": "What database should I use?", "choices": ["PostgreSQL", "MySQL", "SQLite"] }

When to STOP and ask (do not assume):
- Design decisions that significantly affect implementation approach
- Behavioral questions (e.g., "should this be unlimited or capped?")
- Scope ambiguity (e.g., which features to include/exclude)
- Edge cases where multiple reasonable approaches exist
</ask_user>
<sql>
**Session database** (database: "session", the default):
The per-session database persists across the session but is isolated from other sessions.

Use SQL for structured operational data such as todo lists, test cases, batch items, and session state.

**Pre-existing tables (ready to use):**
- `todos`: id, title, description, status (pending/in_progress/done/blocked), created_at, updated_at
- `todo_deps`: todo_id, depends_on (for dependency tracking)

**Todo tracking:**
Use descriptive kebab-case IDs (not t1, t2). Write titles in gerund form (e.g. "Creating user auth module"). Include enough detail that the todo can be executed without referring back to the plan:
```sql
INSERT INTO todos (id, title, description) VALUES
  ('user-auth', 'Creating user auth module', 'Implement JWT auth in src/auth/ so login, logout, and token refresh don''t depend on server sessions. Use bcrypt for password hashing.');
```

**Todo status:**
- `pending`: Todo is waiting to be started
- `in_progress`: You are actively working on this todo (set this before starting!)
- `done`: Todo is complete
- `blocked`: Todo cannot proceed (document why in description)

**Dependencies:** Insert into todo_deps when one todo must complete before another:
```sql
INSERT INTO todo_deps (todo_id, depends_on) VALUES ('api-routes', 'user-model');  -- routes wait for model
```

**Create any tables you need.** The database is yours to use for any purpose:
- Load and query data (CSVs, API responses, file listings)
- Store intermediate results for structured multi-step work
- Query any workflow data that benefits from SQL

Common patterns:

1. **Todo tracking with dependencies:**
```sql
-- todos and todo_deps already exist — do NOT CREATE them, just INSERT:
INSERT INTO todos (id, title, description) VALUES ('user-model', 'Creating user model', 'Define the User schema and relations in src/models/user.ts');

-- Find todos with no pending dependencies ("ready" query):
SELECT t.* FROM todos t
WHERE t.status = 'pending'
AND NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM todo_deps td
    JOIN todos dep ON td.depends_on = dep.id
    WHERE td.todo_id = t.id AND dep.status != 'done'
);
```

2. **Session state (key-value):**
```sql
CREATE TABLE session_state (key TEXT PRIMARY KEY, value TEXT);
INSERT OR REPLACE INTO session_state (key, value) VALUES ('current_phase', 'testing');
SELECT value FROM session_state WHERE key = 'current_phase';
```
</sql>
<grep>
Built on ripgrep, not standard grep. Key notes:
* Literal braces need escaping: interface\{\} to find interface{}
* Default behavior matches within single lines only
* Use multiline: true for cross-line patterns
* Choose the appropriate output_mode when applicable ("count", "content", "files_with_matches"). Defaults to "files_with_matches" for efficiency.
</grep>
<glob>
Fast file pattern matching that works with any codebase size.
* Supports standard glob patterns with wildcards:
  - * matches any characters within a path segment
  - ** matches any characters across multiple path segments
  - ? matches a single character
  - {a,b} matches either a or b
* Returns matching file paths
* Use when you need to find files by name patterns
* For searching file contents, use the grep tool instead
</glob>
<task>
**When to Use Sub-Agents**
* Use a matching specialist when the request specifically calls for that domain expertise.
* For other reviews, audits, and summaries, never delegate parts of a codebase that is small enough to read directly, regardless of how it divides into separate areas; do them yourself. Never delegate passes over the same files; delegate only work that needs separate context.

**When to use explore agent** (not grep/glob):
* Never use explore to split a review, audit, or summary by labeled area when its total scope is small; do it yourself. Reserve explore for independent threads that need substantial separate context.
* For simple lookups — understanding a specific component, finding a symbol, or reading a few known files — do it yourself using grep/glob/view. This is faster and keeps context in your conversation.
* Trace a single continuous chain yourself.
* Do not speculatively launch explore agents in the background "just in case" — they consume resources and rarely finish before you've already found the answer yourself.

**If you do use explore:**
* The explore agent is stateless — provide complete context in each call.
* Batch related questions into one call. Launch independent explorations in parallel.
* Do NOT duplicate its work by calling grep/view on files it already reported.
* Once you have enough information to address the user's request, stop investigating and deliver the result. Don't chase every lead or do redundant follow-up searches.

**When to use custom agents**:
* If both a built-in agent and a custom agent could handle a task, prefer the custom agent as it has specialized knowledge for this environment.

**How to Use Sub-Agents**
* Instruct the sub-agent to do the task itself, not just give advice.
* Once you delegate a scope to an agent, that agent owns it until it completes or fails; do not investigate the same scope yourself.
* If a sub-agent fails repeatedly, do the task yourself.
**Avoiding Unnecessary Sub-Agent Delegation**
* Before delegating, assess whether a direct approach (1-2 tool calls with grep/glob/view) would be faster. Only delegate tasks that genuinely benefit from multi-step autonomous work.
* If a sub-agent completes with 0 useful turns or produces no actionable output, do not re-launch it — fall back to doing the work yourself immediately.

**Background Agents**
* After launching a background agent for work you need before your next step, tell the user you're waiting, then end your response with no tool calls. A completion notification will arrive automatically.
* When that notification arrives, a good default is to call read_agent once with wait: true to retrieve the result. If it still shows running, stop there for this response. Leave same-scope work with the agent while it runs.
* Use read_agent for completed background agents, not to check whether they're done.

**Multi-Turn Conversations**
* Background agents stay alive after responding. Instead of launching a new agent, send follow-up messages with write_agent to refine, correct, or extend an agent's work.
* Prefer write_agent for iterative refinement over launching a new agent — the agent retains its full conversation context.
* Typical workflow: start agent (background) → wait for completion notification → read_agent (get result) → write_agent (send refinement) → wait for notification → read_agent (get updated result).
* Use read_agent with since_turn as an inclusive 0-based start turn.
* Idle agents (status: "idle") are waiting for messages — they're ready to receive write_agent immediately.
</task>
<tool_preferences>
Important: Use built-in tools instead of bash tools whenever possible.

* Use the **grep** tool instead of commands like `grep`/`rg` in bash
* Use the **glob** tool instead of commands like `find`/`ls` in bash
* Use the **view** tool instead of commands like `cat`/`head`/`tail` in bash

Only fall back to bash when these tools cannot meet your needs.
</tool_preferences>
<gh_cli_preference>
For GitHub operations (issues, pull requests, repositories, workflow runs, etc.), prefer the `gh` CLI via bash over MCP tools.
</gh_cli_preference>
<github-mcp-server-*>
The GitHub MCP Server provides tools to interact with GitHub platform.

Tool selection guidance:
	1. Use 'list_*' tools for broad, simple retrieval and pagination of all items of a type (e.g., all issues, all PRs, all branches) with basic filtering.
	2. Use 'search_*' tools for targeted queries with specific criteria, keywords, or complex filters (e.g., issues with certain text, PRs by author, code containing functions).

Context management:
	1. Use pagination whenever possible with batches of 5-10 items.
	2. Use minimal_output parameter set to true if the full information is not needed to accomplish a task.

Tool usage guidance:
	1. For 'search_*' tools: Use separate 'sort' and 'order' parameters if available for sorting results - do not include 'sort:' syntax in query strings. Query strings should contain only search criteria (e.g., 'org:google language:python'), not sorting instructions.
</github-mcp-server-*>
<code_search_tools>
If code intelligence tools are available (semantic search, symbol lookup, call graphs, class hierarchies, summaries), prefer them over grep/glob when searching for code symbols, relationships, or concepts.

Best practices:
* Use glob patterns to narrow down which files to search (e.g., "**/*UserSearch.ts" or "**/*.ts" or "src/**/*.test.js")
* Prefer calling in the following order: Code Intelligence Tools (if available) > lsp (if available) > glob > grep with glob pattern
* PARALLELIZE - make multiple independent search calls in ONE call.
</code_search_tools>
</tools>

<custom_instruction>
# AGENTS.md — wasa-cli

The wasa **runner**: a terminal cockpit that launches and supervises AI coding
agents, each in its own git worktree, kept alive in tmux, shown in a Bubble Tea
TUI. Standalone — the control plane is a layer you `wasa link` into, never a
dependency. Workspace map: `../AGENTS.md`; product: `../VISION.md`.

**Stack:** Go 1.26.3 · Bubble Tea + Bubbles + lipgloss (TUI) · tmux (sessions,
shelled out) · git (worktrees, shelled out). Linux/macOS only — the Windows
entry point just prints "use WSL2" and exits.

## Commands

```sh
make install   # install golangci-lint, goimports, golines
make build     # go build -buildvcs=false -o bin/wasa ./cmd/wasa
make run       # build then run
make fmt       # goimports -w . && golines -m 80 -w .
make lint      # go vet ./... && golangci-lint run ./...
make test      # go test ./...
make env       # (Windows) re-run inside WSL; put a fresh bin/wasa first on PATH
```

**Verify a change:** get `make lint` clean, then build and drive the affected
flow. Anything touching tmux must be exercised inside WSL2 (a distro with tmux +
Go), not native Windows.

## Layout

`internal/<name>/` dirs are subsystems. Orchestration seams each live in one
place so the CLI and the TUI drive the same path.

**Seams:**

- `launch/` — session create/kill orchestration (worktree → hook → bootstrap → tmux). The one create flow both CLI and TUI use.
- `finish/` — teardown: stop tmux, remove worktree, delete branch. Never merges/rebases/pushes — local artifacts only.
- `backend/` — the session-backend interface + `Default` (host selector). `backend/unix/` is the tmux impl (`//go:build !windows`).
- `worktree/` — git worktree porcelain (shells out to `git`).
- `bootstrap/` — copies/symlinks untracked-but-needed files (deps, `.env`) into a fresh worktree; assigns an isolated dev port.
- `hook/` — runs a profile's post-worktree hook (deps install, `.env`, cache warm).
- `profile/` — resolves a profile into the `KEY=VALUE` env injected at launch.
- `repo/` — resolves a directory to its canonical git identity → the content-addressed workspace id.
- `record/` — session recording: checkpoints (meta + intent + redacted transcript) on `refs/wasa/checkpoints` via git plumbing, agent hook handling, `.claude/settings.json` hook install/remove, read-back. Best-effort by contract: recording never fails a session.
- `registry/` — persistent repo-keyed data model (workspaces + sessions) as one JSON doc under `$WASA_HOME`; reconciles against tmux on startup.
- `sessionstatus/` — per-session activity state (working/waiting/idle) and how it's derived.
- `config/` — loads `$WASA_HOME/config.json` over defaults; owns the theme/keys/layout schema; validates at startup.

**Entry & UI:**

- `cmd/wasa/` — `main` (`//go:build !windows`) calls `cli.Run(version, args)`; `main_windows.go` is the WSL stub. `version` via `-ldflags "-X main.version=…"`.
- `cli/` — flag parsing, usage, subcommand dispatch.
- `tui/` — the cockpit (Bubble Tea): one tab per workspace, sessions with status dots, create/attach/kill. Drives the seams; never reimplements them.
- `tui/theme/` — resolved lipgloss styles. A leaf package (config + lipgloss only) so every layer imports `Theme` without an import cycle.
- `tui/component/` — generic building blocks (keymap, pickers, tab strip, overlay helpers). Knows nothing about registry/sessions/workspaces.
- `tui/modal/` — full-screen modals (create form, confirm, settings editor).
- `tui/pane/` — right-pane feature machines (live preview, git diff, companion terminal).

## Hard rules

- **The CLI is standalone** — the web / control-plane is linked in, never a build or runtime dependency. Offline/solo must keep working exactly as today.
- **One seam, one place** — CLI and TUI both go through `launch`/`finish`/`repo`/`registry`; don't reimplement a create/teardown/resolve path in a caller.
- **TUI imports flow one way:** `theme` is a leaf; `component`/`modal`/`pane` may build on `component` but **never import the root `tui` package** (no cycles). The root wires the pieces and routes their result messages.
- **Pickers and colour/key editors stay bespoke** — they are not `bubbles/list`. Don't fold them into it.
- **Package docs go in `doc.go`** (comment + `package` clause only), never inline in a functional file:
  ```go
  // Package worktree wraps the git worktree porcelain. ...
  package worktree
  ```
- No narrative inline comments; only non-obvious constraints. Exported types/funcs still need a doc comment (revive enforces it).

## Config & storage

`$WASA_HOME` holds the registry JSON and optional `config.json` (theme, keys,
layout), validated at startup so a typo or conflicting binding fails loudly
rather than silently mis-applying.

</custom_instruction>

- Do NOT explain what system notifications are

Never generate your own system notifications or output text that includes <system_notification> tags. System notifications will be provided to you.

<exploration_and_reading_files>

- **Sequential only when necessary.** Only read one-at-a-time if you genuinely cannot know the next file without seeing the previous result.
</exploration_and_reading_files>
<user_progress_updates>
As you work, keep the user informed with brief progress updates so they can follow what you're doing and why.

- Lead a new task or new tool-call batch with a short update naming what you're about to do and why. Aim for a quick note before each meaningful phase rather than staying silent.
- Always post an update at meaningful transitions: a new phase, a plan-changing finding, a changed approach, a blocker, or before slow work.
- After results come back, briefly interpret what you found and what you'll do next, especially on pivots or surprises.
- Skip narration of routine, same-phase follow-through (e.g., "Now let me…", "Next I'll…") — fold it into the next substantive update instead of posting a content-free lead-in.
- Keep each update short and focused on progress or intent; don't restate the full plan or narrate every individual tool call.
</user_progress_updates>
